# Hannah Arendt

American political scientist

**Hannah Arendt** (born October 14, 1906, [Hannover](https://www.britannica.com/place/Hannover-Germany) , Germany—died December 4, 1975, [New York](https://www.britannica.com/place/New-York-state) , New York, U.S.) was a German-born American political scientist and philosopher known for her critical writing on Jewish affairs and her study of [totalitarianism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/totalitarianism) .

Arendt grew up in Hannover, [Germany](https://www.britannica.com/place/Germany) , and in Königsberg, Prussia (now Kaliningrad, Russia). Beginning in 1924 she studied [philosophy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/philosophy) at the University of Marburg, the [Albert Ludwig University of Freiburg](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Albert-Ludwig-University-of-Freiburg) , and the University of Heidelberg; she received a doctoral degree in philosophy at Heidelberg in 1928. At Marburg she began a [romantic](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/romantic) relationship with her teacher, [Martin Heidegger](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Martin-Heidegger-German-philosopher) , that lasted until 1928. In 1933, when Heidegger joined the [Nazi Party](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Nazi-Party) and began [implementing](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/implementing) Nazi educational policies as rector of Freiburg, Arendt, who was Jewish, was forced to flee to [Paris](https://www.britannica.com/place/Paris) . She married Heinrich Blücher, a philosophy professor, in 1940. She again became a fugitive from the Nazis in 1941, when she and her husband immigrated to the [United States](https://www.britannica.com/place/United-States) .

Arendt settled in [New York City](https://www.britannica.com/place/New-York-City) and became research director of the Conference on Jewish Relations (1944–46), chief editor of Schocken Books (1946–48), and executive director (1949–52) of Jewish Cultural Reconstruction, Inc., which sought to salvage Jewish writings dispersed by the Nazis. She was naturalized as an American citizen in 1951. She taught at the [University of Chicago](https://www.britannica.com/topic/University-of-Chicago) from 1963 to 1967 and thereafter at the [New School for Social Research](https://www.britannica.com/topic/The-New-School) in New York City.

Britannica Quiz Philosophy 101

Arendt’s reputation as a major political thinker was established by her _[Origins of Totalitarianism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Origins-of-Totalitarianism)_ (1951), which also treated 19th-century [antisemitism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/antisemitism) , [imperialism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/imperialism) , and [racism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/racism) . Arendt viewed the growth of [totalitarianism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/totalitarianism) as the outcome of the disintegration of the traditional nation-state. She argued that totalitarian regimes, through their pursuit of raw political power and their neglect of material or utilitarian considerations, had revolutionized the social structure and made contemporary politics nearly impossible to predict.

_The Human Condition_ , published in 1958, was a wide-ranging and systematic treatment of what Arendt called the _vita activa_ (Latin: “active life”). She defended the classical ideals of work, citizenship, and political action against what she considered a debased [obsession](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/obsession) with mere welfare. Like most of her work, it owed a great deal to the philosophical style of Heidegger.

In a highly controversial work, _Eichmann in Jerusalem_ (1963), based on her reportage of the [trial](https://www.britannica.com/topic/trial-law) of the Nazi war criminal [Adolf Eichmann](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Adolf-Eichmann) in 1961, Arendt argued that Eichmann’s crimes resulted not from a wicked or depraved character but from sheer “thoughtlessness”: he was simply an ambitious [bureaucrat](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/bureaucrat) who failed to reflect on the enormity of what he was doing. His role in the mass extermination of Jews epitomized “the fearsome, word-and-thought-defying banality of evil” that had spread across Europe at the time. Arendt’s refusal to recognize Eichmann as “inwardly” evil prompted fierce denunciations from both Jewish and non-Jewish [intellectuals](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/intellectuals) . The controversy was revived some four decades after Arendt’s death with the publication of Bettina Stangneth’s _Eichmann vor Jersualem: das unbehelligte Leben eines Massenmörders_ (2011; Eichmann Before Jerusalem: The Unexamined Life of a Mass Murderer, 2014), which was based in part on sources not available to Arendt. It challenged the “banality of evil” characterization, arguing that Eichmann had long been a confirmed antisemite.

Arendt resumed contact with Heidegger in 1950, and in subsequent essays and lectures she defended him by claiming that his Nazi involvement had been the “mistake” of a great philosopher. In the late 20th century, following the publication of a volume of letters between Arendt and Heidegger written between 1925 and 1975, some scholars suggested that Arendt’s personal and [intellectual](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/intellectual) attachment to her former teacher had led her to adopt a [lenient](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/lenient) [assessment](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/assessment) of him that was inconsistent with her condemnation of the collaboration of others and with her insistence in various writings that any act of compromise with evil is wholly immoral.

Arendt’s other works include _Between Past and Future_ (1961), _On Revolution_ (1963), _Men in Dark Times_ (1968), _On Violence_ (1970), and _Crises of the Republic_ (1972). Her unfinished manuscript _The Life of the Mind_ was edited by her friend and correspondent [Mary McCarthy](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Mary-McCarthy) and published in 1978. _Responsibility and Judgment_ , published in 2003, collects essays and lectures on [moral](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/moral) topics from the years following the publication of _Eichmann in Jerusalem_ .

This article was most recently revised and updated by Mindy Johnston .
